expectations.text: Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
expectations.evidence: Section '1. Problem & context' covers consumer app, MAU, marketing deadline

expectations.text: Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
expectations.evidence: Section '2. Scope' has explicit 'In scope' and 'Out of scope' subsections

expectations.text: Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
expectations.evidence: Section '3. High-level approach' covers code lifecycle, redemption attribution, credit issuance with ASCII diagram

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
expectations.evidence: Section '4. Detailed design' has 10 subsections (4a-4j) covering API surface, data access, latency, fraud, etc.

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
expectations.evidence: Section '5. Alternatives considered' lists 6 alternatives each with rejection rationale (own microservice, Stripe Coupons, deterministic hashids, server-side attribution, synchronous credit, vendor)

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs / Non-functional requirements section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section '6. Non-functional requirements' includes 99.9% monthly availability, p95 120ms read/200ms write, 500 RPS burst, 4M MAU at 12 months

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section '7. Risks & failure modes' enumerates 9 risks with likelihood, blast radius, mitigation, recovery

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' / 'Migration' section
expectations.evidence: Section '8. Rollout & migration' has Phase 0-3 timeline, backwards compat, rollback story

expectations.text: Contains an 'Observability' / 'Operations' section
expectations.evidence: Section '9. Observability & operations' enumerates pages/tickets/daily, plus Observability NFR subsection with metrics, logs, traces, alerts table

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
expectations.evidence: Section '10. Open questions' has 10 entries, 3 marked [BLOCKER]

expectations.text: Contains no fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems (only placeholders or PRD-mentioned references are acceptable)
expectations.evidence: Only placeholder '<link to referrals runbook — to be added>' and a path reference to the PRD file; no invented company URLs

expectations.text: TDD is structured as an API serving design (mentions endpoints/routes/methods, response shapes, AuthN/Z model)
expectations.evidence: Section 4a has full endpoint table with Method/Path/Auth/Purpose; section 4b explicitly covers AuthN (JWT) and AuthZ (row-level)

expectations.text: Defines a data model / schema for referral codes and redemption events with primary keys and important indexes
expectations.evidence: Section 4c includes SQL DDL for referrals.codes, referrals.redemptions, referrals.credit_receipts, referrals.stats with PRIMARY KEY and explicit indexes (redemptions_referrer_idx, redemptions_status_idx)

expectations.text: Explicitly addresses idempotency of the redemption call (idempotency key, dedup table, conditional write, or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: Redemption idempotent on PK (referee_user_id), INSERT ... ON CONFLICT pattern; credit_receipts table with idempotency_key column 'referral:activate:{redemption_id}:referrer'

expectations.text: Addresses fraud / self-referral / multi-account abuse prevention (referenced as design concern, not punted)
expectations.evidence: Section 4h 'Fraud guardrails' lists 4 explicit rules: self-network (device/IP/payment match), velocity per referrer, velocity per IP/device, trial-abuse signal

expectations.text: Defines or constrains what 'activation' means (e.g., first paid subscription start) and the credit-issuance trigger flow
expectations.evidence: Section 4g 'Credit issuance' details consumer for subscription.activated event with 5-step flow: lookup, fraud check, transactional update, ledger call, ack

expectations.text: Addresses code-collision / generation strategy (random + uniqueness check, deterministic from user ID, etc.)
expectations.evidence: Section 4c: 'secrets.token_bytes mapped to the alphabet and rely on the UNIQUE constraint to detect collisions; on conflict we retry up to 5 times. After 5 retries we widen to 7 chars'

expectations.text: Has a concrete latency budget for the API (numbers, not 'fast')
expectations.evidence: Section 4d: 'p95 ≤ 120 ms end-to-end for GET /referrals/me, p95 ≤ 200 ms for POST /v1/referrals/redemptions' with per-stage table (TLS 10ms, JWT 3ms, Redis 2ms, Postgres 20ms)

expectations.text: Discusses integration with Stripe and the internal credit ledger, including what happens if the credit write fails
expectations.evidence: Section 4g step 4-5 and 4i: 'If step 4 fails after step 3 committed: the receipts table records intent; on next event-redelivery (or manual retry via runbook) we re-issue using the same idempotency key'; ledger row in dependency table

expectations.text: Document is at least 1500 words
expectations.evidence: Document is approximately 4500+ words spanning 10 substantial sections

expectations.text: Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
expectations.evidence: Section '1. Problem & context' describes polling waste, lag, and driver (holiday ramp)

expectations.text: Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
expectations.evidence: Section '2. Scope' has both 'In scope' (Kafka consumer, read API, DLQ, etc.) and 'Out of scope' (order service replacement, cross-order aggregations, etc.)

expectations.text: Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
expectations.evidence: Section '3. High-level approach' describes the Kotlin service on GKE with consumer + read API, includes prose diagram

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
expectations.evidence: Section '4. Detailed design' has 11 subsections (4a-4k) covering broker model, delivery semantics, offsets, DLQ, concurrency, back-pressure, state, schema evolution, data model, read API, dependencies

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
expectations.evidence: Section '5. Alternatives considered' covers Spanner, Postgres, DynamoDB, Redis, Kafka Streams/ksqlDB, scaling order service, and doing nothing — each with verdict

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section '6. Non-functional requirements' has concrete numbers: read API p99<50ms, end-to-end p95<2s, consumer lag p95<30s, DLQ rate <0.01%

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section '7. Risks & failure modes' has 8 risks with likelihood, blast radius, mitigation, recovery columns

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' / 'Migration' section
expectations.evidence: Section '8. Rollout & migration' has 4 phases plus rollback and data backfill plans

expectations.text: Contains an 'Observability' / 'Operations' section
expectations.evidence: Section '9. Observability & operations' plus extensive Observability subsection in §6 covering metrics, logs, traces, alerts

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
expectations.evidence: Section '10. Open questions' has 10 entries with [BLOCKER]/[IMPORTANT]/[NORMAL] severity

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: Uses placeholders like '<link to runbook — to be added>' and only references the PRD path; no invented internal URLs

expectations.text: TDD is structured as an event-driven / streaming consumer design
expectations.evidence: Header explicitly says 'Workload category: Event-driven / streaming consumer (Kafka)' and design is centered on Kafka consumer group with partition-level workers

expectations.text: Explicitly states delivery semantics (at-least-once + idempotent processing) and what makes effects idempotent
expectations.evidence: Section 4b states 'At-least-once delivery. Duplicates happen on producer retry...' and defines effectively-once via event_version + conditional CAS write: 'if event.version <= P.applied_version: drop (duplicate)'

expectations.text: Discusses per-partition ordering and partition key choice (order_id) and what that buys
expectations.evidence: Section 4b: 'Within one partition, messages are strictly ordered... Since partitioning is by order_id, all events for a single order land on one partition and are therefore ordered'

expectations.text: Specifies a consumer lag SLO with a concrete number
expectations.evidence: 'Consumer lag SLO: p95 lag < 30s. p99 < 2min. Alert at p95 > 60s for 5min, page at p95 > 5min'

expectations.text: Specifies a DLQ for bad transitions / poison messages with trigger and replay path
expectations.evidence: Section 4d has table of DLQ triggers (schema_incompatible, illegal_transition, missing_version, gap_not_closed, apply_failed, store_write_failed), DLQ envelope schema, and replay tool 'ops/replay-order.sh order_id'

expectations.text: Specifies offset commit strategy (after processing / batch / at-least-once semantics)
expectations.evidence: Section 4c: 'Auto-commit off. Manual commit after successful apply (or after DLQ). Commit cadence: per message... offsets are committed only after the conditional write succeeds'

expectations.text: Takes an actual position on storage (DynamoDB vs Postgres vs Redis) with stated tradeoffs, not punted
expectations.evidence: Section 5: Explicitly recommends Spanner over DynamoDB ('Not recommended on GCP-only'), Postgres ('Viable second choice'), Redis ('Rejected as primary store'). Section 3 states 'Recommendation: Spanner'

expectations.text: Addresses Avro schema evolution under BACKWARDS compatibility
expectations.evidence: Section 4h: 'Schema Registry mode: BACKWARDS (already enforced). Adding a new optional field is always safe. Removing a field needs a forward migration... Breaking change → new topic orders.events.v2'

expectations.text: Addresses out-of-order event handling within partition (sequence numbers / event-timestamp last-writer-wins / state-machine guards)
expectations.evidence: Section 4b defines event_version monotonic per order_id, with gap buffer: 'if event.version > P.applied_version + 1: park event in a per-order pending buffer... do NOT commit Kafka offset for this message yet — wait for the gap to close'

expectations.text: Designs back-fill / full-replay path for initial seed and recovery
expectations.evidence: Section 8 'Data backfill: before Phase 3, run a one-time full backfill: bump the consumer group offset to earliest, let it grind through all retained history (~15min at 24 partitions in parallel). Conditional-write CAS makes this safe to repeat.' Plus replay tool in §4d

expectations.text: Designs the GET /orders/{id}/projection read endpoint with latency target
expectations.evidence: Section 4j: 'GET /orders/{order_id}/projection. Single Spanner point read by primary key. p99 < 10ms expected; p99 < 50ms SLO has comfortable headroom.' Response includes applied_version, last_event_time, 404 handling, auth

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section 6 includes concrete numbers: p95 run < 5 min, 2x poll_cadence freshness, $500/month budget alert, 26h Vendor K staleness

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section 7 'Risks & failure modes' has a table with 12 risks (R1-R12) with likelihood, blast radius, mitigation, recovery

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' / 'Migration' section, addressing the shadow-mode period from the PRD
expectations.evidence: Section 8 'Rollout & migration' includes 6-week phasing table and 'Shadow mode mechanics' with shadow_mode flag per vendor

expectations.text: Contains an 'Observability' / 'Operations' section

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: Only placeholder '<link to confluence runbook — to be added>' and example domain inventory-k@vendors.<our-domain> with explicit placeholder

expectations.text: TDD is structured as a scheduled pull-based processing design
expectations.evidence: Section 0 explicitly states 'Dominant pattern: scheduled pull-based processing' referring to references/tdd-scheduled-pull.md

expectations.text: Explicitly acknowledges and handles all 3 ingestion mechanisms (REST, SFTP, email) — does not collapse them into 'all REST'
expectations.evidence: Sections 4c.1 (REST adapter, 8 vendors), 4c.2 (SFTP adapter, 3 vendors), 4c.3 (Email adapter, Vendor K) each have distinct treatments

expectations.text: Proposes a per-vendor connector / adapter pattern (or equivalent abstraction) with shared core
expectations.evidence: Common 'InventorySource' interface with three adapter implementations; pull stages (fetch, normalise, diff, apply, audit) shared across all 12 vendors

expectations.text: Calls out the Vendor F 60 req/hour rate limit and how the design respects it
expectations.evidence: Section 4f dedicated to Vendor F: hourly cadence not 30-min, ≤55 calls per run with 5 headroom, chunked full-refresh across multiple hours

expectations.text: Discusses per-vendor cursor / watermark / 'since' state, and how it's stored and recovered
expectations.evidence: Section 4b 'Cursor / watermark' defines vendor_sync_state table with last_cursor jsonb, adapter-specific cursor semantics, and recovery via S3 raw payloads

expectations.text: Addresses no-overlap / locking between scheduled runs
expectations.evidence: Section 4d: 'Per-vendor lock: Redis SET NX PX', UUID-fenced; second Beat fire becomes no-op with skipped_due_to_lock_total metric

expectations.text: Defines a freshness SLO and per-vendor stale-data alerting (24h+ stale = alarm)
expectations.evidence: NFR target: 'last_success_at no older than 2 × poll_cadence (e.g. 60 min for a 30-min vendor; 26h for Vendor K). Breach = page on-call' via vendor_staleness_minutes metric

expectations.text: Addresses storage and rotation of per-vendor credentials
expectations.evidence: Section 4c.1 and 6/Security: 'stored in AWS Secrets Manager, one secret per vendor. Rotated per vendor's own policy; rotation is operator-driven, no automatic rotation in v1'

expectations.text: Has a concrete plan for the emailed-CSV vendor (e.g., dedicated mailbox, automated parser, quarantine for malformed)
expectations.evidence: Section 4c.3: dedicated SES inbound address, DMARC/SPF/DKIM enforcement, S3 archival, SES messageId dedup, quarantine to vendor-k-needs-triage/ S3 prefix, 26h staleness alarm

expectations.text: Designs the shadow-mode rollout phase (compare to current source of truth, dispute resolution)
expectations.evidence: Section 8 Shadow mode mechanics: shadow_mode=true per vendor, applied=false audit rows, daily disagreement report compared with Google Sheet, ops arbitrates; Open Q2 names the arbiter

expectations.text: Designs the audit log of inventory changes with source and timestamp
expectations.evidence: Section 4g defines InventoryAudit table with run_id, vendor_id, internal_sku, old_qty, new_qty, source_observed_at, applied_at, monthly partitions

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: No invented internal URLs; only references to incident IDs (INC-4421), Sigstore cosign, CycloneDB, and channels like #devtools/#oncall

expectations.text: TDD is structured as a CLI utility design
expectations.evidence: Header explicitly states 'Workload category: CLI utility'; design centers on command surface, exit codes, STDIN, distribution

expectations.text: Sketches the command surface with flags / args / --help shape
expectations.evidence: Section 4a includes a full sketched --help with Usage, Arguments, Flags (--format, --schema-hint, --max-memory, --temp-dir, --explain, etc.), and Exit codes

expectations.text: Defines exit codes (incl. any tool-specific codes as a deliberate design choice)
expectations.evidence: Section 4b defines exit codes 0-5 with table; explicit design stance to NOT overload exit code with 'no rows found' unlike grep

expectations.text: Documents STDIN / pipe composition behavior (reads stdin when no files; works in pipelines)
expectations.evidence: Section 4c: 'cat file.jsonl | logsql' works; 'Interactive TTY with no input → exit 2'; mixed stdin + files documented

expectations.text: Explicitly states streaming vs whole-load posture, with memory bound for aggregations (GROUP BY / ORDER BY)
expectations.evidence: Section 4d: 'Non-aggregating queries: ≤ 512 MB resident'; 'Aggregating queries: ≤ --max-memory resident (default 4 GB); spills to disk beyond'

expectations.text: Specifies output formats and how the user selects between them (--json/--csv/--tsv/etc.)
expectations.evidence: '-f, --format <json|csv|tsv|table>' flag with TTY-aware default (json when piped, table when TTY)

expectations.text: Takes an explicit position on Go vs Python with stated tradeoffs (does not punt to 'either is fine')
expectations.evidence: Section 5: 'Chosen: Go' with reasoning — single static binary, broken python toolchains, cross-compilation, DuckDB wheel size; Python rejected explicitly

expectations.text: Takes an explicit position on SQL parser strictness with reasoning
expectations.evidence: Section 4f: 'Decision: hand DuckDB the SQL... Strict by DuckDB's standards, not by ANSI's; no forgiving mode' with stated reasons against lenient parsing

expectations.text: 'Alternatives considered' includes existing tools like jq, duckdb, awk, or similar — with honest rejection rationale
expectations.evidence: Section 5 explicitly compares DuckDB (chosen) vs SQLite vs custom vs Apache DataFusion; also Do-nothing alternative discussing status quo jq|grep|awk chains

expectations.text: Explicitly omits NFRs that do not apply to a CLI (availability SLO, multi-region, disaster recovery) — states the omission rather than fabricating values
expectations.evidence: Section 6 has 'NFRs explicitly omitted' subsection: 'Availability SLO: N/A — it's a CLI'; 'Disaster recovery: N/A — no persistent state'; 'Multi-region: N/A — local tool'

expectations.text: Addresses the 5s/1GB-compressed performance target with a real plan
expectations.evidence: Performance NFR: 'Target: time-to-first-result < 5 s on a 1 GB gzipped input on 2024-era M-series MBP'; mechanism is DuckDB streaming scan with vectorised execution; verification via 'make bench' nightly with > 20% regression failure

expectations.evidence: Section 3 states 'Delivery semantics: at-least-once. Duplicates are guaranteed; the consumer must be idempotent.' Section 6.3 details event_id dedup + last_applied_sequence CAS as idempotency mechanisms

expectations.text: Discusses per-partition ordering and partition key choice (order_id) and what that buys
expectations.evidence: Section 3 notes 'topic is keyed by order_id and a single key always lands on a single partition; Kafka guarantees per-partition order.' Section 6.4 enforces one worker per partition

expectations.text: Specifies a consumer lag SLO with a concrete number
expectations.evidence: NFR-2 freshness p95 < 2s with alert at 1.5s; NFR-3 'consumer lag < 30s' availability condition

expectations.text: Specifies a DLQ for bad transitions / poison messages with trigger and replay path
expectations.evidence: Section 6.6 defines orders.events.dlq.v1 with reason taxonomy (invalid_transition, sequence_gap_timeout, cas_storm, decode_error, unknown_event_type); replay path via §8 tool

expectations.text: Specifies offset commit strategy (after processing / batch / at-least-once semantics)
expectations.evidence: Section 6.7 'enable.auto.commit=false; we commit synchronously after successful store write'; Section 6.3 step 6 'Commit the Kafka offset only after the write succeeds'

expectations.text: Takes an actual position on storage (DynamoDB vs Postgres vs Redis) with stated tradeoffs, not punted
expectations.evidence: Section 7.3 explicitly recommends Cloud Spanner as primary, rejects DynamoDB (cross-cloud), and identifies Cloud SQL Postgres as fallback if cost vetoed

expectations.text: Addresses Avro schema evolution under BACKWARDS compatibility
expectations.evidence: Section 11 'Schema evolution': 'Input events: governed by Confluent SR with BACKWARDS compat. The consumer is built against the latest schema; old events deserialize cleanly because new fields are optional with defaults.'

expectations.text: Addresses out-of-order event handling within partition (sequence numbers / event-timestamp last-writer-wins / state-machine guards)
expectations.evidence: Section 6.2 mandates per-order_id monotonic sequence number; Section 6.3 uses sequence for staleness drop and CAS guard; Section 6.5 introduces park buffer for gaps

expectations.text: Designs back-fill / full-replay path for initial seed and recovery
expectations.evidence: Section 8 covers single-order replay (8.1) and full backfill (8.2) with shadow-table approach and 2h throughput target

expectations.text: Designs the GET /orders/{id}/projection read endpoint with latency target
expectations.evidence: Section 9 specifies the endpoint contract with auth, JSON response shape, 404/503 semantics, staleness headers; NFR-1 sets p99 < 50ms latency target

expectations.text: A single TDD-*.md file exists in the outputs/ directory
expectations.evidence: File TDD-vendor-inventory-sync.md exists in outputs/ directory.

expectations.text: Document is at least 1500 words
expectations.evidence: Document spans 828 lines with substantive content across 14 sections; well over 1500 words.

expectations.text: Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
expectations.evidence: Section '1. Problem statement' explains stale inventory and $80K/month oversold incidents.

expectations.text: Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
expectations.evidence: Section 2 has 'In scope (this project)' and 'Explicitly out of scope (per PRD)' subsections.

expectations.text: Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
expectations.evidence: Section '3. High-level approach' with pipeline diagram and key shape decisions.

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
expectations.evidence: Section '4. Detailed design' covers components, ingestion adapters, parsing, normalization, diff, sanity gates, apply, and staleness detection.

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
expectations.evidence: Section 7 table lists 7 alternatives (one adapter per vendor, Step Functions/Airflow, direct DB pull, no S3 staging, manual paste, asking Vendor K to switch to SFTP, webhook ingress) each with rejection rationale.

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section 8 NFRs with concrete numbers: 99.5% availability, <=15 min REST freshness, <=30 min SFTP/email, <$300/mo cost, 140K SKUs capacity.

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section '9. Failure modes and how the design handles them' has detailed table of 15+ failure scenarios.

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' / 'Migration' section, addressing the shadow-mode period from the PRD
expectations.evidence: Section 10 'Rollout and migration' with Phase 3 dedicated to '2 weeks from PRD' shadow mode and rollback strategy.

expectations.text: Contains an 'Observability' / 'Operations' section
expectations.evidence: Section 8.6 covers Observability (Datadog metrics, logs, tracing, alerts) and Section 11 covers Operations (runbooks, reconciliation, weekly digest).

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
expectations.evidence: Section 13 'Open questions' has 11 numbered items including catalog API surface, shadow-mode arbitration, Vendor F rate-limit strategy.

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: Only generic placeholders like s3://inventory-sync-raw/, vendor-k-inventory@inventory.ourdomain.com, and REST paths like /catalog/inventory; no fabricated internal URLs.

expectations.text: TDD is structured as a scheduled pull-based processing design
expectations.evidence: Section 3 explicitly states 'Pull-only' approach with Celery Beat scheduler driving per-vendor cadence; pipeline stages from ingest to apply.

expectations.text: Explicitly acknowledges and handles all 3 ingestion mechanisms (REST, SFTP, email) — does not collapse them into 'all REST'
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2 has distinct subsections 4.2.1 REST adapter (8 vendors), 4.2.2 SFTP adapter (3 vendors), 4.2.3 Email adapter (Vendor K); each with tailored design.

expectations.text: Proposes a per-vendor connector / adapter pattern (or equivalent abstraction) with shared core
expectations.evidence: Defines IngestionAdapter Protocol with three implementations; 'Three ingestion adapters, one downstream pipeline. REST/SFTP/Email adapters differ only in how they produce a raw payload... Everything after that is shared code.'

expectations.text: Calls out the Vendor F 60 req/hour rate limit and how the design respects it
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2.1: 'Vendor F's 60 req/hour cap means we have to choose... Default plan: 2x daily full pulls for Vendor F, gives us ~30 reqs each, comfortably under cap with headroom for retries.' Plus per-vendor token bucket in Redis.

expectations.text: Discusses per-vendor cursor / watermark / 'since' state, and how it's stored and recovered
expectations.evidence: VendorRun table stores per-run state and raw_s3_keys; pagination 'streamed to S3 page-by-page... vendor's pagination state is recoverable'; Open Q5 asks about updated_since support; payload_dedupe ledger by SHA-256.

expectations.text: Addresses no-overlap / locking between scheduled runs
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2.1: 'Per-vendor concurrency: at most 1 in-flight run per vendor (enforced via SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on VendorRun or a Redis lock).' Also failure-modes row on operator double-trigger.

expectations.text: Defines a freshness SLO and per-vendor stale-data alerting (24h+ stale = alarm)
expectations.evidence: Section 2 sets concrete SLOs (<=15 min REST, <=30 min SFTP/email); Section 4.8 Staleness detection runs every 15 min comparing against vendor.staleness_threshold (default 24h); Section 8.6 alerts sev2 when vendor_freshness_seconds > 24h.

expectations.text: Addresses storage and rotation of per-vendor credentials
expectations.evidence: Section 8.5 Security: 'Vendor credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, fetched at task start, never written to logs. config JSONB stores references to secrets, not secret values. SFTP: prefer key-based auth; keys provisioned per vendor.'

expectations.text: Has a concrete plan for the emailed-CSV vendor (e.g., dedicated mailbox, automated parser, quarantine for malformed)
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2.3: dedicated mailbox vendor-k-inventory@inventory.ourdomain.com via SES inbound, SNS->SQS->Celery, acceptance rules (from-domain allowlist, subject regex, attachment types), quarantine bucket for failures with ops Slack ping, manual override path; ClamAV scanning.

expectations.text: Designs the shadow-mode rollout phase (compare to current source of truth, dispute resolution)
expectations.evidence: Phase 3 details 2-week shadow mode: 'Daily comparison report: today, new system would have changed X SKUs; Google Sheet recorded Y changes; here are the deltas. Ops reviews.' Plus apply_enabled config flag and Open Q2 for arbitration.

expectations.text: Designs the audit log of inventory changes with source and timestamp
expectations.evidence: inventory_audit table: 'Append-only audit of every change actually pushed to catalog' with run_id, vendor_id, sku, old_on_hand, new_on_hand, applied_at, source_observed_at. 2-year retention; DB user has INSERT, SELECT only.

expectations.text: A single TDD-*.md file exists in the outputs/ directory
expectations.evidence: TDD-logsql-cli.md exists in outputs/ directory

expectations.text: Document is at least 1500 words
expectations.evidence: Document is substantially longer than 1500 words, with 15 numbered sections, code blocks, tables, and detailed prose across ~680 lines

expectations.text: Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'Problem statement' describes the on-call workflow pain and INC-4421

expectations.text: Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Scope' includes 'In scope (v0.1)' and 'Out of scope (v0.1)' subsections

expectations.text: Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'High-level architecture' with pipeline diagram (Sources -> Decoder -> Project -> Aggregator -> Encoder)

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
expectations.evidence: Section 5 'Detailed design' covers module layout, type system, SQL grammar EBNF, source resolution, JSON decoding strategy, aggregation, sort, encoders, config, error reporting

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
expectations.evidence: Section 10 covers Language (Go/Python/Rust/DuckDB/q-jq-dsq), Parser (ANTLR/goyacc/PEG/Pratt), Schema, and Execution model alternatives each with verdicts and rationale

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section 9 NFRs with concrete targets: <5s for 1GB compressed, ~4.2s budget breakdown, mem-limit 1GiB default, fuzz >=1 hour per release, 90% invocations <10s

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section 12 'Risks & failure modes' is a table with 8 risks, likelihoods, impacts, and mitigations

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' section appropriate for a CLI (release / distribution / versioning)
expectations.evidence: Section 13 'Rollout & migration' has Phase 0-3 with v0.1 alpha, v0.5 beta, v1.0 GA, Homebrew tap, signed/notarized macOS, SLSA-2 Linux releases, rollback via pinned versions

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
expectations.evidence: Section 11 'Open questions' lists 8 items including JSON decoder choice, LIKE semantics, HLL precision, timestamp inference, brew tap location

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: No invented internal URLs; only generic references like 'devtools/homebrew-tap' and standard GitHub releases

expectations.text: TDD is structured as a CLI utility design
expectations.evidence: Section 7 'CLI contract' with full flag list, positional args, exit codes; explicitly designed as a stateless CLI

expectations.text: Sketches the command surface with flags / args / --help shape
expectations.evidence: Section 7 shows full flag list: -f/--format, -o/--output, --mem-limit, --max-line-size, --on-bad-row, --explain, --explain-analyze, -c/--config, -v/--verbose, --version, -h/--help

expectations.text: Defines exit codes (incl. any tool-specific codes as a deliberate design choice)
expectations.evidence: Section 8 exit codes table: 0 success, 1 runtime, 2 usage, 3 resource limit, 130 SIGINT; explicitly rejects 'exit 1 if zero rows' as a deliberate choice

expectations.text: Documents STDIN / pipe composition behavior (reads stdin when no files; works in pipelines)
expectations.evidence: Section 5.4: 'If no positional args: read STDIN. If STDIN is a TTY and no files: error'; Section 9.7 documents line-buffered vs fully-buffered pipe mode; appendix A4 shows gunzip|logsql pipeline

expectations.text: Explicitly states streaming vs whole-load posture, with memory bound for aggregations (GROUP BY / ORDER BY)
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2 Memory model: 'Streaming scan -> filter -> project is O(1) memory'; 'GROUP BY and ORDER BY are bounded by --mem-limit (default 1 GiB)' with spill to disk

expectations.text: Specifies output formats and how the user selects between them (--json/--csv/--tsv/etc.)
expectations.evidence: Section 5.8 table of formats (json/csv/tsv/table), selected via -f/--format flag with auto default (table if TTY, ndjson otherwise)

expectations.text: Takes an explicit position on Go vs Python with stated tradeoffs (does not punt to 'either is fine')
expectations.evidence: Section 3.1 'Language: Go' - explicit decision with rationale around 60-second install promise, pip UX, perf budget; Python rejected explicitly in 10.1 alternatives table

expectations.text: Takes an explicit position on SQL parser strictness with reasoning
expectations.evidence: Section 3.2 'Parser strictness: Forgiving but typed' - hand-written Pratt parser, forgiving lexing but strict semantics, with explicit rejection of both 'strict ANSI' and 'looks like SQL'

expectations.text: 'Alternatives considered' includes existing tools like jq, duckdb, awk, or similar — with honest rejection rationale
expectations.evidence: Section 10.1 includes DuckDB CLI ('Heavy dep ~50 MB, wrong defaults for ndjson logs') and q/jq/dsq ('q is CSV-first, jq isn't SQL, dsq requires schema and isn't streaming')

expectations.text: Explicitly omits NFRs that do not apply to a CLI (availability SLO, multi-region, disaster recovery) — states the omission rather than fabricating values
expectations.evidence: Section 9.3 Reliability says 'No daemon, no state' but does not explicitly state DR/availability SLO/multi-region as 'not applicable'. While section 6 notes statelessness, there's no explicit omission statement like 'DR: not applicable; this is a CLI'

expectations.text: Addresses the 5s/1GB-compressed performance target with a real plan
expectations.evidence: Section 9.1 has a detailed budget breakdown table: gzip 2.0s, line scan 0.4s, JSON tokenize 1.5s, filter+count 0.3s, output 0.01s, total ~4.2s with 15% headroom; plus pprof/benchstat plan and pgzip fallback
